text: Yes.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So this is also a library that I open sourced in 2020.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I think it's very clear that I was excited about Compose because all these projects...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Kept going out one after the other.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It was just me being in the zone, so to speak.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so Showcase is something that I open searched with Airbnb.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it was based on a project that I saw internally.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So I take no credit for the idea itself.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But I applied that to Compose.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And there were a few reasons.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So anytime there is a declarative system, and I think the most obvious example, and probably the first example is React, it's very easy to create new components.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so when you're alone...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: you will probably create 10 20 components and that's your app now scale that up to maybe 100 engineers which some some of the companies do have that many engineers focused on just android by the way over time you will end up in a situation where you have thousands and thousands of components
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And one of your goals hopefully at that point is that we want people to reuse as much as possible.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe you have a design system that you want to somehow highlight.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so the solution to that problem that most companies have is that they'll create some kind of a component library that you can browse through on some debug setting in the app.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And every company is maintaining this component browser or your UI element browser.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: manually either that or they might create some tooling which over time automates some things but the perspective I had was that why should 103 companies create 103 variants of the same thing and so it was very clear that if not me someone else is going to build this and so I started going down the rabbit hole with most of my projects they usually start as me wanting to learn something and so the only reason I started Jetpack Compose.app was to learn React that was my only goal I had no other goal
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that was interesting because I had gotten comfortable with Compose.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so I understood what declarative UI was.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I wanted to apply it to a new system because my hypothesis was that, you know what, it might be easier for me to pick it up because I understand the mental model needed.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: With Showcase, my goal was to understand annotation processing and to create something that uses annotation processing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so this felt like a good way of working on something cool, but also picking up some new skills that I had not experimented with before.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And thus, Showcase was born.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so just like a one-line spiel of Showcase will be, Showcase is an annotation processor-based library that lets you visualize, discover, and highlight Jetpack Compose UI elements in your code base.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so you can think of it as a component browser where you can see all the components that you've created in your entire repository.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You need very little effort to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so what Compose created was something that they called previews, which let you preview something in Android Studio, which is amazing, by the way, slightly flaky.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But let's assume that they fix those problems, which I'm very bullish that all those problems will be fixed over time.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I try to make sure that I add first class support for it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if you have previews already set up, showcase supports that by default.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you need no additional work in order to see all your components in a single place.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What preview allows you to do is preview those components in a single file, but it does not give you a snapshot of your entire repo.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Plus it does not let you search through all your components.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if I just want to type, I don't know, avatar card, I can't very easily do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Nor can I see all the styles associated with it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Showcase makes these things easy.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That is pretty slick.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It is quite the...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So it's basically like, hey, let's get preview, but let's get it across multiple components and make it easy to sort of like quickly see what these components specifically are.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: their approach was to rewrite the entire UI toolkit from the ground up.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this is a very pure rewrite.

text: And so...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you think about it, like it had been almost 10 to 12 years since Android APIs were being developed and specifically focusing on the UI toolkit, inheritance was something that was prevalent.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so every view essentially extended the view.java file or the view class rather, which lives in the view.java file, which is a 30, 40,000 line class.

text: And so automatically you can...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: imagine that no matter how hard you try to optimize something, you are getting a ton of work that's probably not needed for a given view.

text: So that's one.

text: Inheritance also makes things hard to refactor and you need to spend a lot of time doing that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.

text: And so ideally, you just want to make sure that you are passing the data that a component needs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And as an example, let's say that you have a data class called news metadata that has 30 properties.

text: Instead of passing this entire data class to a component, you will only pass what it needs.

text: And so if you're just passing the fields it needs, one, most likely you are relying on primitives.

text: If not, that's fine too.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But most likely you're relying on some primitives.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Maybe you're just toggling the visibility or something.

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay.

text: And so you're...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: just letting the state know through an event, but you're not actually changing the state in the view itself.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You want to change the state in a single place.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so that's where the single source of truth and unidirectional data flow comes in this conversation.

text: any other declarative system whether you compare it with flutter or react compose is able to redraw just a single composable without having to redraw its parent or even its subtree and so what tends to happen in the other view systems is that the runtime or whatever is powering this mechanism will find the smallest subtree that needs to be redrawn and it will redraw that smallest subtree
speaker: SPEAKER_01
